1: vayyashkem Yehoshua baboqer — "Joshua rose early in the morning." The waw-consecutive imperfect signals sequential narrative past; the early rising (hashkem) is a recurring marker of urgent, divinely-directed action throughout Joshua (3:1; 6:12; 7:16; 8:10). Shittim to the Jordan bank: the final staging camp before the crossing. The people yalinu sham lifnei aboram — lodged there before crossing — a further three-day wait (cf. 1:11).
2: miqets shloshet yamim vayyaaveru hashoterim beqerev hamachaneh — "after three days the officers passed through the midst of the camp." The three-day structure closes; the moment of crossing has arrived. The officers' circuit through the camp is the same administrative procedure as ch. 1:10-11 — Joshua's command structure operates through shotrim (officers/scribes).
3: keraotekhem et aron berit YHWH Elohekhem vehakohanim haLeviyim nosim oto — "when you see the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God carried by the Levitical priests." The ark's appearance is the signal for the march. The full title — ark of the covenant of YHWH your God — emphasizes the covenant, the divine name, and the people's relational identity as "your God." The ark had led in wilderness marching (Num 10:33); now it leads the land-entry.
4: ach rachov yihyeh veineikhem uveno alfahim ba'ammah — "but there shall be a distance of about two thousand cubits between you and it." Two thousand cubits (approximately 1 km) — a buffer zone of reverence. Lo tiqrevu eilav — do not come near it. Lema'an tishdeu et haderekh asher telechu vah — so you may know the way to walk in — the ark's lead position makes it the pathfinder for ground the people have never entered.
5: hithqaddeshu ki machar ya'aseh YHWH beqirbekhem nifla'ot — "consecrate yourselves, for tomorrow the LORD will do wonders among you." Hithqaddeshu is the hitpael of qadash — reflexive sanctification, preparing oneself for encounter with the holy. The wonders (nifla'ot) are announced before they occur, so that when they happen Israel knows the source. Nifla'ot is the word for the plagues and the Exodus miracles — Joshua uses it to frame the Jordan crossing as a new Exodus event.
6: seu et aron haberit veivru lifnei ha'am — "take up the ark of the covenant and pass on before the people." The ark's front position is the key spatial arrangement: it enters the river first, demonstrating that YHWH goes ahead. The priests are the ark's carriers, maintaining the Levitical requirement (Num 4:15; Deut 10:8). The physical arrangement embodies theology: YHWH in the vanguard, the people following.
7: bayyom hazeh achel gedallekha be'einei kol Yisrael — "today I will begin to make you great in the sight of all Israel." Gadal (piel: to make great, to magnify) is the same verb used for YHWH's exaltation of the patriarchs. Ba'asher heyiti im Moshe ehyeh imakh — the promise of ch. 1:5 returns: as I was with Moses I will be with you. The Jordan crossing will be Joshua's investiture — the moment all Israel sees that YHWH's presence has passed to him.
8: ke'amdekhem al qetzeh mei hayarden taamodun — "when you stand at the edge of the Jordan's waters, stand firm." Amadon (qal imperfect) — the priests are to take their stand at the water's edge. They must step in before the miracle occurs. The sequence matters: obedient entry into the water precedes the divine response. This is not magic but faith-action — the miracle is attached to the priests' prior obedience, not to any ritual formula.
9: geshu hena ushme'u et divrei YHWH Eloheikhem — "come close here and hear the words of the LORD your God." The summons to draw near (geshah) and listen (shema') precedes the miraculous announcement. The Sinai pattern is inverted: at Sinai the people kept their distance from the divine presence; here they are summoned to approach and hear. The Jordan crossing begins with a congregational sermon, not a military order.
10: be'zot tede'un ki El chai beqirbekhem — "by this you will know that the living God is among you." El chai — the living God — is a divine title that emphasizes divine vitality and power in contrast to the dead idols of Canaan. The Jordan crossing is designed as a theodicy: the nations of Canaan (vv. 10-11 lists seven) will see that the God who acts is the living God, not a regional deity with limited power.
11: hineh aron berit Adon kol ha'arets — "the ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth." Adon kol ha'arets — Lord of all the earth — is a title that asserts YHWH's universal sovereignty over the land about to be entered. The Canaanite claim to the land is implicitly invalidated: the one who owns all the earth is disposing of a portion of his own property according to his own covenant purposes.
12: ve'attah qechu lakhem shnei asar anashim — "now take for yourselves twelve men, one from each tribe." The twelve men are appointed before the crossing, their purpose initially unstated. The number twelve (one per tribe) signals a representatively complete act: what these men do will be on behalf of all Israel. Their specific task (taking stones) is explained after the crossing in ch. 4.
13: veha'ya kenuach kapot raglei hakohanim nosay aron YHWH Adon kol ha'arets bemei hayarden — "when the feet of the priests who carry the ark of the LORD of all the earth rest in the waters of the Jordan." The verb nuach (to rest, plant) is the same word used for Elijah's spirit resting on Elisha and for the Spirit resting on the Messiah (Isa 11:2). The feet of the ark-bearers resting in the water triggers the cutting off of the upstream flow: yikratun (they will be cut off) from krt — the same word for covenant-making (karat berit), covenant-cutting.
14: vayyehi binso'a ha'am me'oholeihem la'avor et hayarden — "when the people broke camp to cross the Jordan." The waw-consecutive narration accelerates: the people break camp, the priests carry the ark ahead, they approach the Jordan. The participial phrase vehakohanim nosim et ha'aron berit lifnei ha'am (the priests carrying the ark of the covenant before the people) emphasizes the spatial arrangement throughout the action.
15: keha'aron kanahalei ha'ayovim — "as those who carry the ark came to the Jordan and the feet of the priests carrying the ark were dipped (nitbelu) in the edge of the water." The Jordan was in flood (male al gevotav kol yemei qatsir — full to its banks all the days of harvest). The harvest-flood detail is precise: late March/April barley harvest is when the Jordan swells from snowmelt in Hermon. The priests enter at the most dangerous moment, when the river is highest. Faith is maximally tested before it is maximally confirmed.
16: vayaamedu hamayim hayordim — "the waters coming down stood." The verb amad (stand, stop) is the same word used for taking a stand in battle. The waters, which had been flowing, take their stand — a reversal of nature. Nema rabba meod ad Adam ha'ir asher mitslad Tsaretan — they piled up in a heap at Adam, a town near Zarethan. The water-wall at Adam is geographically specific: about 16-17 miles upstream from the crossing point, near the confluence with the Jabbok. The downstream waters tammu nikhratu — were completely cut off.
17: vayaamedu hakohanim nosim et aron berit YHWH beyabasha betokh hayarden hakun — "the priests who carried the ark of the covenant of the LORD stood firm on dry ground in the midst of the Jordan." Yakhin (firm/established) — the same root as the name of one of Solomon's temple pillars (1 Kgs 7:21). The dry-ground crossing repeats the Exodus sea-crossing pattern (Exod 14:22, 29); the repetition is structurally deliberate. The new generation entering the land is a new Exodus generation, born of the same divine act.
